mitre_technique_name: Archive Collected Data: Archive via Utility
anomaly_signals: 7zip_from_persistence: 7z.exe spawned by the malicious scheduled task (MicrosoftEdgeUpdateTaskMachineUA) established on Day 3
password_protected_archive: -p@dm1n2022 creates encrypted archive — blocks forensic inspection without password
encrypt_header: -mhe=on also encrypts archive headers — even filenames hidden from analysis
patient_data_target: source is \\AIIMS-FILE-SRV-02\patients\ — directly targeting patient records
staging_to_temp: C:\Windows\Temp\~logs\ uses tilde prefix to mimic Windows temp/logs — evasion technique
late_night_execution: 23:45 IST — off-hours execution when monitoring staff reduced
recursive_flag: -r flag means ALL subdirectories recursively — complete patient database exfiltration
ground_truth_note: MALICIOUS: The attacker is archiving the entire patient records file share. The command reveals the attacker's sophistication: (1) password-protected with encrypted headers so incident responders can't read the archive without the password '@dm1n2022', (2) recursive flag captures all nested directories, (3) staging to C:\Windows\Temp\~logs\ uses a tilde-prefixed folder name mimicking system temp folders. Most critically, 7z.exe is spawned BY the malicious scheduled task created on Day 3 — the forensic chain is clear. This event, if detected, would have revealed the Day 3 persistence mechanism and enabled full rollback.

anomaly_signals: massive_write: 47.3GB written to single file — 100x larger than any baseline write operation to Temp directory
sustained_3h_write: consistent write rate for 3+ hours — automated bulk operation
temp_directory_staging: large data accumulation in Temp — staging for exfiltration
write_rate_spike: 40-48 MB/s sustained — saturates local storage and creates detectable I/O pattern
no_legitimate_reason: no business process writes 47GB encrypted archives to Windows\Temp at midnight
ground_truth_note: MALICIOUS: 47.3GB written to a single encrypted 7z file over 3 hours at midnight. This is the data collection phase — the attacker has compressed 47GB of patient records (AIIMS treats ~3500+ patients daily, 30 years of records = enormous PII dataset). The 3-hour write window (23:45 to 02:52) combined with the 40+ MB/s sustained write rate would be visible in storage I/O metrics on any SIEM with endpoint telemetry. This represents complete patient database exfiltration preparation.

new_permissions: SYSTEM: Full Control only — all other ACEs removed
anomaly_signals: restrictive_acl: denying Everyone (S-1-1-0) from the staging directory — prevents incident responders from reading archive
post_write_lockdown: permissions changed immediately after 7z.exe completes — deliberate cover-your-tracks sequence
icacls_by_system: SYSTEM account directly manipulating ACLs via command line is highly anomalous
evidence_protection: attacker protecting staged data from discovery
ground_truth_note: MALICIOUS: Immediately after the 47GB archive completes, the attacker restricts directory permissions using icacls. The command denies Read/Execute to Everyone (S-1-1-0) and grants Full Control only to SYSTEM — meaning even domain admins cannot browse the staging directory without SYSTEM-level tools. This is a deliberate anti-forensics step: if a sysadmin browses C:\Windows\Temp\, they'll get 'Access Denied' on the ~logs folder, potentially dismissing it as a system folder. This technique buys the attacker time before exfiltration.

anomaly_signals: shadow_copy_deletion: destroys all Volume Shadow Copies — eliminates file recovery options
quiet_flag: /quiet suppresses confirmation dialogs — automated silent destruction
pre_exfil_cleanup: executed after data staging — attacker eliminating recovery options before exfiltrating data
vssadmin_by_system: VSS admin operations by SYSTEM from cmd.exe chain is anomalous
irreversible_action: shadow copy deletion is irreversible — data cannot be recovered without external backup
ransomware_precursor: VSS deletion is near-universal in ransomware deployment playbooks
ground_truth_note: MALICIOUS: vssadmin delete shadows /all /quiet destroys ALL Windows Volume Shadow Copies — the primary local recovery mechanism for Windows servers. This action is irreversible and completely eliminates in-place file recovery options. Crucially, this occurs on Day 7/8 — 15 days before the ransomware deploys. The attacker is methodically eliminating recovery options in advance. This single event, detected by EDR, would have been a 100% confidence alert — there is NO legitimate operational reason to silently delete all shadow copies on a domain controller. This event alone should have triggered an automated incident response.
